name: Alcohol Use Disorder
overview: Alcohol Use Disorder (AUD) is a chronic medical condition defined by an impaired ability to stop or control alcohol use despite adverse consequences. AUD exists on a continuum from mild to severe and is one of the most common substance use disorders treated in Kentucky. Effective treatment pairs medical withdrawal management with evidence-based therapy and, where indicated, FDA-approved medication.
signs: Drinking more, or for longer, than intended
Wanting to cut down but being unable to
Strong cravings or urges to drink
Drinking interfering with work, school, or family obligations
Continuing to drink despite relationship, legal, or physical-health consequences
Giving up important activities to drink or recover from drinking
Needing more alcohol to achieve the same effect (tolerance)
Experiencing withdrawal symptoms when alcohol wears off

symptoms: elevated blood pressure and heart rate
Delirium tremens (DTs) risk window: confusion, agitation, fever, severe autonomic instability
Acute symptoms resolve; protracted withdrawal (sleep disruption, mood changes) may persist for weeks

treatmentApproach.title: Medical detox
treatmentApproach.detail: Alcohol withdrawal can be life-threatening. Medically supervised detox with benzodiazepine taper and vitals monitoring is the standard of care for moderate-to-severe AUD.

treatmentApproach.title: Residential treatment
treatmentApproach.detail: After stabilization, 21–28 days of residential treatment addresses the behavioral and psychological components of AUD in a structured, sober environment.

treatmentApproach.title: Medication-Assisted Treatment
treatmentApproach.detail: FDA-approved medications — naltrexone (oral or Vivitrol), acamprosate, and disulfiram — reduce cravings and relapse risk when paired with therapy.

treatmentApproach.title: Step-down to PHP, IOP, outpatient
treatmentApproach.detail: Structured step-down care maintains clinical contact during the highest-risk post-residential period.

localContext: Kentucky's rate of excessive alcohol use and alcohol-related deaths remains above the national average. Louisville's urban and suburban communities both report high demand for AUD treatment, and Kentucky Medicaid covers the full ASAM continuum — including medically supervised detox — for members meeting medical-necessity criteria.
medicaidCoverageSummary: All six Kentucky Medicaid MCOs cover alcohol detox, residential treatment, PHP, IOP, outpatient, and MAT for AUD. Prior authorization is typical for residential admissions; detox is usually authorized urgently based on clinical presentation.

faqs.q: Is it safe to detox from alcohol at home?
faqs.a: Moderate-to-severe alcohol withdrawal can cause seizures or delirium tremens and is potentially fatal. For anyone with a history of heavy or long-term use, medically supervised detox is strongly recommended.

faqs.q: Does Kentucky Medicaid cover alcohol rehab?
faqs.a: Yes. All Kentucky Medicaid MCOs cover medical detox, residential treatment, PHP, IOP, outpatient services, and FDA-approved medications (naltrexone, acamprosate, disulfiram) when medically necessary.

faqs.q: How long does alcohol rehab take?
faqs.a: Medical detox typically runs 3–7 days. Residential treatment commonly runs 21–28 days, followed by PHP (2–6 weeks) and IOP (8–12 weeks). Duration is individualized based on severity and progress.
